title: Personnel Monitoring Devices and Dose Units
narrationScript: A survey meter protects the crew in the moment. Personnel monitoring protects you across a career. It is the running account of what your body has actually received, shot by shot, week by week, year by year, and it is the legal record that backs you up if anything is ever questioned. To use it properly you first need the language of dose, so we start there. Get one distinction absolutely straight, the difference between dose and dose rate. Dose rate is how fast you are receiving radiation, right now. It is a speedometer, read in milliroentgen per hour or sieverts per hour. Dose is the accumulated total, the odometer, read in milliroentgen or sieverts with no per hour attached. A survey meter reads the speedometer. Your dosimeter reads the odometer. Confuse them and every calculation you do afterward is wrong. Multiply a dose rate by time in the field and you get dose. That is the whole relationship. Now the units, and you need both the older system and the modern international one, because both are still on instruments and paperwork. Exposure describes ionization produced in air. The traditional unit is the roentgen. The international unit is the coulomb per kilogram, a direct measure of electric charge freed in a kilogram of air. One roentgen is about a quarter of a thousandth of a coulomb per kilogram. Absorbed dose describes energy actually deposited in material, including your tissue. The traditional unit is the rad. The international unit is the gray, one joule of energy per kilogram, and one gray equals one hundred rad. Dose equivalent folds in how damaging the radiation type is, by multiplying absorbed dose by a quality factor. The traditional unit is the rem. The international unit is the sievert, and one sievert equals one hundred rem. For the gamma and X radiation of everyday radiography the quality factor is one, so as a working approximation one roentgen of exposure gives about one rad absorbed and about one rem of dose equivalent. For neutrons that shortcut fails completely, because the quality factor is around ten, and that is exactly why dose equivalent exists. Next, how the record actually gets made. On a typical radiography job you wear monitoring on the trunk of your body, between neck and waist, in the same spot every day, facing the work, never buried under a tool vest and never clipped to a boot. The classic combination is a whole body badge that makes the permanent record, a direct reading pocket dosimeter you can check on the spot, and an alarming ratemeter that yells when the field jumps. This lesson focuses on the recording devices, the badge and the pocket dosimeter. The pocket dosimeter is a small quartz fiber electroscope shaped like a pen. Charging it moves a fine fiber to zero on an internal scale. Radiation ionizes the air in its chamber, the charge bleeds off, and the fiber climbs the scale. Hold it up to the light, look through the eyepiece, and you read your accumulated dose immediately, no lab required. The standard type reads gamma and X radiation, commonly over a range of zero to two hundred milliroentgen. Around neutron sources the standard instrument is the wrong instrument. A gamma dosimeter barely responds to neutrons and will happily read near zero while you absorb real neutron dose. Neutron work uses neutron monitoring dosimeters, devices built or lined so neutron interactions register, along with neutron capable badges such as albedo or track etch designs. Film badges came first and are still around. A packet of photographic film rides in a holder behind a set of small filters. Radiation darkens the film, the darkness is measured in a laboratory, and the filter pattern even reveals something about the radiation type and energy. Film is cheap and it leaves a permanent developed record, but it fades with time, and heat and humidity can fog it into uselessness. The thermoluminescent dosimeter, the TLD, replaced film almost everywhere. Its small crystals trap energy when irradiated, and when the processing lab heats them they release that energy as light, in proportion to your dose. TLDs are more accurate, reusable, and far more tolerant of field conditions, though reading one erases it, so there is no rereadable original. Many programs now use optically stimulated luminescence badges, a similar idea read out with light instead of heat. Treat whichever badge you wear like the legal instrument it is. Keep it on you, keep it out of hot trucks, never share it, never wear someone else's. A lost, damaged, or unusually exposed badge goes to the radiation safety officer immediately. The exact devices, ranges, and exchange schedules come from your license and your written procedures, and they vary by jurisdiction. Next lesson we put these devices to work, reading them, logging them, and acting when one goes off scale.
imagePrompt: Close-up of a radiographer clipping a pen-style pocket dosimeter and a whole-body dosimetry badge to the chest pocket of work coveralls inside an equipment trailer, survey meter and alarming ratemeter on the bench behind.

sections.heading: Dose Versus Dose Rate
sections.body:
Personnel monitoring exists to answer one question with legal precision: **how much radiation has this person actually received?** To use it, keep two quantities separate:

- **Dose rate** — how fast radiation is being delivered *right now*. The speedometer. Units carry **per hour**: mR/h, mrem/h, Sv/h. This is what a survey meter or alarming ratemeter reads.
- **Dose** — the accumulated total. The odometer. Units carry **no time**: mR, rem, Sv. This is what a pocket dosimeter or badge records.

The two connect through time: **dose = dose rate × time**. Stand in a 10 mR/h field for half an hour and your dosimeter climbs 5 mR. A high dose rate for seconds can be a small dose; a low rate all shift long can be a large one — which is why a quiet ratemeter does not excuse you from reading the dosimeter.

Mixing these up is not a beginner's slip; it causes real overexposures, as when a boundary calculated from a *dose* number is treated as safe for an unlimited *time*. Check the units on every instrument and every procedure line: if it says per hour, it is a rate; if not, it is a total.

sections.heading: The Units: Coulomb per Kilogram, Gray, and Sievert
sections.body:
Three distinct physical ideas get measured, each with a traditional unit and an SI unit — and both systems still appear on instruments, badges, and reports, so you must move between them fluently.

- **Exposure** — ionization produced **in air** by gamma or X radiation. Traditional unit: the **roentgen (R)**. SI unit: the **coulomb per kilogram (C/kg)** — literally the electric charge liberated per kilogram of air. 1 R = 2.58 × 10⁻⁴ C/kg. Most survey meters and pocket dosimeters are scaled in roentgen-family units.
- **Absorbed dose** — energy actually deposited **in material**, including tissue. Traditional unit: the **rad**. SI unit: the **gray (Gy)**, one joule per kilogram. **1 Gy = 100 rad**.
- **Dose equivalent** — absorbed dose **weighted for biological damage** by the quality factor (Q): dose equivalent = absorbed dose × Q. Traditional unit: the **rem**. SI unit: the **sievert (Sv)**. **1 Sv = 100 rem**.

For the gamma and X radiation of routine radiography, Q = 1 and tissue absorbs nearly what air ionization implies, so **1 R ≈ 1 rad ≈ 1 rem** is the standard working approximation. For neutrons (Q ≈ 10) and alpha taken internally (Q ≈ 20) the shortcut fails — which is precisely why dose equivalent exists as its own quantity.

sections.caption: From ionization in air to biological risk: exposure, absorbed dose, and dose equivalent, with the conventional and SI unit for each step.
Radiation quantities and units — conversions every radiographer should know cold.
rows: Ionization in air (γ and X only)
Energy deposited per kilogram of material
Fails for neutrons (Q ≈ 10) and internal alpha (Q ≈ 20)

sections.heading: Wearing Monitoring Badges Correctly
sections.body:
A dosimeter only records the dose where it sits, so **where and how you wear it is part of the measurement**. Typical practice for radiography:

- Wear whole-body monitoring **on the trunk, between neck and waist** — chest pocket or collar area is standard — in the **same location every day**, so readings are comparable.
- Keep it **facing the work**, on the side of your body toward the source, and never buried under a tool vest, lead, or a jacket that was not part of the calibration.
- Badges are **personal**: never wear another worker's badge, never lend yours, and never let a badge ride along on a job you are not on.
- **Store monitoring at a designated low-background location** (the control point) when off shift — not in the truck cab with the exposure device, not on the dashboard in the sun.
- Protect it from heat, moisture, and crushing, all of which corrupt the reading; a fogged film badge or a cooked TLD defends no one.
- A **lost, damaged, or unexpectedly exposed badge goes to the RSO immediately** so your dose can be reconstructed and a replacement issued.

Deliberately exposing a badge to falsify a record is a serious violation in any jurisdiction — the badge is a legal instrument, and it is treated like one.

sections.heading: Pocket Dosimeters: Gamma, X-Ray, and Neutron Types
sections.body:
The **direct-reading pocket dosimeter** is the device that tells you your dose *right now*, in the field, with no laboratory in the loop. Inside the pen-sized barrel is a small air-filled **ionization chamber** containing a delicate **quartz fiber electroscope**. Charging the dosimeter places the fiber at **zero** on an internal scale. As radiation ionizes the air in the chamber, the stored charge leaks away and the fiber climbs the scale in proportion to accumulated dose. Hold the eyepiece toward any light source and read the fiber's position directly.

- **Gamma and X-ray dosimeters** are the standard radiography type, commonly scaled **0–200 mR**, matched to the daily doses expected under a well-run program. (Higher-range models exist for emergency use.)
- **Neutron monitoring dosimeters** exist because the standard instrument **barely responds to neutrons** — a gamma dosimeter can sit near zero while real neutron dose accumulates. Neutron-capable designs register neutron interactions (for example through hydrogenous or boron-bearing chamber construction), and neutron work also calls for neutron-capable badges such as **albedo TLDs** or **track-etch** detectors. Around any neutron source, carrying only gamma instruments means working blind.

The pocket dosimeter's virtues are immediacy and simplicity; its weaknesses are modest accuracy, a limited range, and vulnerability to shock — drop one and a jarred fiber can read falsely until re-zeroed and checked. It supplements the badge; it never replaces it.

sections.caption: Cutaway of a direct-reading pocket dosimeter: the quartz fiber moves across an internal scale as ionization in the chamber bleeds away the stored charge.

sections.heading: Film Badges and Thermoluminescent Dosimeters
sections.body:
The **badge** is the device of record — processed at required intervals (commonly monthly or quarterly) by an accredited laboratory, and the basis of your official dose history.

- **Film badges** hold a packet of photographic film behind a set of small **filters** (open window, plastic, aluminum, cadmium or lead). Radiation darkens the film; measured optical density gives the dose, and the pattern of darkening behind different filters indicates radiation type and approximate energy. Film is inexpensive and leaves a **permanent developed record**, but it fades over time, saturates at high doses, and is easily fogged by **heat, humidity, and light leaks** — a badge left on a dashboard can read a dose its wearer never received.
- **Thermoluminescent dosimeters (TLDs)** use crystals — commonly lithium fluoride — that trap energy in their lattice when irradiated. The processing lab **heats** the chip, and it releases the stored energy as **light in proportion to dose**. TLDs are more accurate than film, reusable, nearly tissue-equivalent, and far more tolerant of field conditions. Their trade-off: **readout erases the chip**, so there is no re-readable original, only the recorded result. Albedo-type TLDs extend the technology to neutron monitoring.
- Many services now issue **optically stimulated luminescence (OSL)** badges — similar physics, read out with light, and re-readable.

Whichever you wear, the badge is the **legal dose of record**; the pocket dosimeter is the immediate field check. The two are partners, and lesson rs-1-4 covers the daily routine that ties them together.

sections.title: The badge protects you — if you protect it
sections.body: Your badge is the evidence that your employer's program kept you within limits — and your evidence if it did not. Wear it every working day, in the same place on your trunk, facing the work. Never store it near the exposure device or in a hot vehicle, and report a lost or damaged badge to the RSO **the day it happens**, not at exchange time. Exact device combinations, ranges, and exchange intervals are set by your license and written procedures and vary by jurisdiction.

figures.title: Exposure, absorbed dose, and dose equivalent

checkQuestions.question: Your survey meter reads 50 mR/h and your pocket dosimeter reads 50 mR. What is the difference between these two readings?
options: Nothing — they are the same measurement in different packages
The meter shows the rate radiation is being delivered right now; the dosimeter shows the total you have accumulated
The dosimeter shows a rate; the meter shows an accumulated total
The meter reading is always exactly double the dosimeter reading
checkQuestions.explanation: Dose rate (mR/h) is the speedometer — how fast dose is arriving — while dose (mR) is the odometer of accumulated exposure; they connect through dose = rate × time.

checkQuestions.question: The SI unit coulomb per kilogram (C/kg) is the modern replacement for which quantity and unit?
options: Absorbed dose, replacing the rad
Exposure, replacing the roentgen
Dose equivalent, replacing the rem
Activity, replacing the curie
checkQuestions.explanation: Exposure is ionization produced in air by gamma or X radiation, traditionally measured in roentgen; the SI equivalent measures the liberated charge directly, with 1 R = 2.58 × 10⁻⁴ C/kg.

checkQuestions.question: An absorbed dose of 2 gray equals how many rad — and 0.05 sievert equals how many rem?
options: 20 rad and 50 rem
200 rad and 0.5 rem
200 rad and 5 rem
2 rad and 5 rem
checkQuestions.explanation: 1 Gy = 100 rad, so 2 Gy = 200 rad; 1 Sv = 100 rem, so 0.05 Sv = 5 rem — the same factor of 100 links both unit pairs.

checkQuestions.question: Typical correct wear of whole-body personnel monitoring during radiography is:
options: Clipped inside the tool bag that travels with you
On the trunk between neck and waist, in the same location daily, facing the work
On the ankle, where dose rates are highest when cranking
Left at the control point so it cannot be damaged in the field
checkQuestions.explanation: The badge records dose only where it sits — trunk placement between neck and waist, worn consistently and facing the source side of the work, makes it represent whole-body dose.

checkQuestions.question: Which statement correctly contrasts a film badge with a TLD?
options: A TLD develops like photographic film and fades within days
A heated TLD chip releases stored energy as light in proportion to dose, and the chip is reusable; film leaves a permanent developed record but is vulnerable to heat and humidity
A film badge can be re-zeroed in the field; a TLD cannot be processed at all
Film badges respond only to neutrons, while TLDs respond only to gamma
checkQuestions.explanation: TLD readout heats the crystal and measures emitted light (erasing the chip for reuse), while film is developed and measured for darkening — a permanent record, but one easily fogged by environment.

references: CP-105-2024 Radiation Safety Outline §1.3.1–1.3.2 (personnel monitoring devices and units)
SI and conventional unit relationships: 1 R = 2.58 × 10⁻⁴ C/kg; 1 Gy = 100 rad; 1 Sv = 100 rem
Typical NRC / Agreement-State personnel monitoring expectations, described generically — your license and procedures govern
